participants.extracted: The trial enrolled 3220 Burkinabe infants and young children aged 8.8 to 9.9 months from rural communities in the Dandé Health District in southwestern Burkina Faso. All were permanent residents of the area, had written parental consent, and were eligible for the study if they did not have severe anemia (Hb <50 g/L), severe malnutrition (weight-for-length <70% of the NCHS/WHO median), bipedal edema, serious illness, congenital abnormalities affecting growth, chronic medical conditions requiring frequent medical care, known HIV infection in the infant or mother, peanut allergy, prior anaphylaxis/serious allergic reaction, or concurrent participation in another clinical trial. Of those enrolled, 2435 children were assigned to the intervention cohort and 785 to the non-intervention cohort; among the intervention cohort, children were randomized to one of four zinc-related SQ-LNS/tablet regimens.

interventions.extracted: Children in the intervention cohort were randomized from 9 to 18 months of age to one of four daily supplement regimens: 1) small-quantity lipid-based nutrient supplement (SQ-LNS) without added zinc plus placebo tablet (LNS-Zn0), 2) SQ-LNS containing 5 mg zinc plus placebo tablet (LNS-Zn5), 3) SQ-LNS containing 10 mg zinc plus placebo tablet (LNS-Zn10), or 4) SQ-LNS without zinc plus a 5 mg zinc dispersible tablet (LNS-TabZn5). All SQ-LNS products were otherwise identical and provided about 20 g/day (118 kcal/day) with protein, fat, iron, and multiple micronutrients; caregivers were instructed to give the daily ration mixed with food in two separate meal times. The zinc tablets were to be dissolved in water or breast milk and given once daily, not with other foods. In addition, children in the intervention cohort received free community-based treatment for illness episodes, including oral rehydration salts for diarrhea, paracetamol for fever, and antimalarial treatment for positive malaria tests, with referral for severe illness. Children in non-intervention communities did not receive SQ-LNS, tablets, or illness-treatment services during 9 to 18 months, but were offered SQ-LNS after study follow-up was completed.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure growth and morbidity outcomes in young Burkinabe children from 9 to 18 months of age. Primary outcomes were physical growth, incidence of diarrhea, incidence of malaria, and plasma zinc concentration (pZn). Growth was assessed using length-for-age z-score (LAZ), weight-for-age z-score (WAZ), and weight-for-length z-score (WLZ), with stunting, underweight, and wasting defined respectively as LAZ, WAZ, and WLZ below -2 SD. Anthropometric measures included length, weight, mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC), and head circumference (HC) at baseline (9 months) and follow-up at 18 months, with additional anthropometry at 12 and 15 months in the intervention cohort. Morbidity outcomes included diarrhea, defined as ≥3 loose or liquid stools per 24 hours, and malaria, defined as reported or confirmed fever with a positive rapid diagnostic test; these were monitored throughout the 9-month intervention period. Fever was also recorded as reported fever and confirmed fever (>37.5°C). Biochemical outcomes included hemoglobin (Hb), pZn, zinc protoporphyrin (ZPP), and inflammatory markers C-reactive protein (CRP) and alpha-1-acid glycoprotein (AGP), measured at baseline and again at 18 months in a venous blood sub-sample, with pZn and ZPP adjusted for inflammation. Mortality was also planned to be compared between enrollment and the end of the study.
